skill.name: error-debugging-error-trace
skill.description: Guides implementation of error tracking, structured logging, alerting, error grouping, and observability workflows for production applications.
skill.summary: Build practical error monitoring and incident triage workflows for production systems.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, network, environment, and blocker patterns. Review found no prompt injection or malicious behavior, and most command and blocker detections are false positives from Markdown code fences, template literals, or non-security examples. The remaining risk is medium because the playbook includes examples that send error events, user context, request metadata, and alert payloads to configured monitoring services.

medium_findings.title: Telemetry Sent To Configured Monitoring Endpoints
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The playbook includes examples that post serialized error events to a configurable endpoint with a bearer API key and send alert payloads to a Slack webhook. This is expected for observability tooling, but it can expose stack traces, request details, or operational data if endpoint configuration and redaction are weak.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls are explicit and semantically part of error tracking. The risk depends on deployment configuration, so this is a legitimate medium-risk pattern rather than confirmed exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: User And Request Metadata May Be Captured
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The examples add configured user identifiers, user segments, custom user context, IP address, URL, and user agent data to monitoring or logging records. The skill also warns against logging secrets, but implementers must apply privacy controls before production use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly collect user and request metadata. This is common for observability, but it has clear privacy and compliance impact if copied without minimization.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Detections Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported Ruby or shell backtick detections correspond to Markdown code fences, documentation links, or JavaScript and JSX template literals. No evidence found that the skill itself executes shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations were checked in context and are documentation syntax or string interpolation inside examples. I did not find executable shell invocation behavior in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker And Environment Detections Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The reported environment and blocker lines do not show secret access, weak cryptography, or reconnaissance. Line 364 is a sensitive-key redaction list, line 372 redacts matching keys, SKILL.md line 38 warns against logging secrets, and the hash example uses SHA-256 for non-security error grouping.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is defensive redaction guidance or non-security fingerprinting. No evidence found of weak cryptography used for authentication, encryption, or integrity protection.

low_findings.title: Codebase Analysis Examples Read Local Project Files
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The playbook includes analyzer examples that traverse project files and read package or source files to inspect error handling and logging. This is expected for the skill, but users should scope analysis to intended repositories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem reads are explicit and relevant to code analysis. The risk is low because they are local examples and no hidden upload path is tied to these reads.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound Error Event Posting
dangerous_patterns.description: Example code posts serialized error events to a configurable endpoint using an authorization header. This pattern needs endpoint allowlisting, redaction, and rate controls in production.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The outbound POST and bearer token header are directly visible. The pattern is dangerous only if configuration or sanitization is unsafe.

dangerous_patterns.title: Collection Of User And Request Context
dangerous_patterns.description: Example code attaches user and request metadata to error events and logs. This can help triage incidents but may process personal data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly include user and request fields in monitoring context. Privacy impact depends on the deployed data policy.

content.value_statement: Production errors are hard to triage when logs, alerts, and traces are scattered. This skill helps design error tracking, structured logging, alert routing, grouping, and dashboards.
actual_capabilities: Assesses current error capture, logging, alerting, and grouping practices.
Provides implementation patterns for Sentry, custom error tracking, and structured logging.
Defines alert rules, severity levels, cooldowns, and routing workflows.
Suggests error grouping, deduplication, dashboard, and real-time stream designs.
Includes safety guidance for redacting secrets and limiting production sampling.
limitations: Does not provide a ready-to-run monitoring service by itself.
Requires users to supply valid service credentials and deployment details.
Example integrations must be adapted to each application framework.
Privacy, retention, and compliance settings need human review before production use.

use_cases.title: Add Production Error Tracking
use_cases.description: Instrument an application with captured exceptions, stack traces, releases, environments, and safe user context.

use_cases.title: Improve Alert Quality
use_cases.description: Create severity rules, cooldowns, and routing so on-call teams receive actionable alerts.

use_cases.title: Standardize Debugging Workflows
use_cases.description: Align logs, dashboards, grouping, and triage steps across services and teams.

prompt_templates.title: Review Current Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Review our current error monitoring setup and list the main gaps in capture, alerting, grouping, and logging. Keep recommendations practical.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Sentry Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Sentry integration for this application. Include initialization, sampling, environment tags, redaction, and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design Alert Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Create alerting rules for production errors. Include severity levels, thresholds, cooldowns, routing channels, and noise reduction controls.

prompt_templates.title: Build Observability Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Propose an end-to-end error observability architecture for multiple services. Cover logging, tracing, grouping, dashboards, alerting, retention, and privacy controls.

output_examples.input: Our API logs exceptions but engineers miss repeated production failures.
output_examples.output: A prioritized rollout plan covering exception capture, structured log fields, alert thresholds, grouping rules, ownership tags, and test-error validation.

output_examples.input: We need better alerts for checkout failures without paging on every single error.
output_examples.output: A severity matrix with rate-based thresholds, cooldown periods, affected-user checks, Slack routing, and escalation rules for sustained incidents.

output_examples.input: We are adding user context to monitoring but need to reduce privacy risk.
output_examples.output: A redaction checklist for tokens, cookies, request bodies, personal data, sampling rules, and retention settings before enabling production capture.

best_practices: Redact secrets, cookies, tokens, request bodies, and unnecessary personal data before sending events.
Use sampling, grouping, and cooldowns to reduce alert noise during high-volume incidents.
Validate monitoring with controlled test errors before relying on alerts in production.
anti_patterns: Do not send raw request payloads or credentials to monitoring providers.
Do not page teams for every low-value exception without grouping or thresholds.
Do not enable automatic recovery actions without clear safety limits and audit logs.

faq.question: What does this skill help implement?
faq.answer: It helps implement error capture, structured logging, alerting, grouping, dashboards, and triage workflows.

faq.question: Does it require Sentry?
faq.answer: No. It includes Sentry examples, but it also covers custom trackers and general observability patterns.

faq.question: Can it analyze an existing codebase?
faq.answer: Yes. The playbook includes examples for inspecting error handling, logging libraries, and monitoring setup.

faq.question: Is it safe for production systems?
faq.answer: It can be used safely when users apply redaction, sampling, access controls, and endpoint review.

faq.question: What data needs special care?
faq.answer: Treat stack traces, request metadata, user identifiers, tokens, cookies, and payloads as sensitive.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: Backend engineers, SREs, platform teams, and engineering leads responsible for production reliability can use it.
